text:
Suggested Questions

  • What color do you think will most likely occur? (Ask this question often to have students focus on the experimental data changing as more data are added.)

As more and more students take a turn, ask the following questions:

  • Are the data that are accumulating useful for making your prediction? Explain. (As data are accumulated, you begin to see patterns that help in making predictions.)
  • What is the experimental probability of choosing each color? (Results will vary. Make sure students understand that the experimental probability will change as the game goes on.)
  • What do you need to know to find the theoretical probability of drawing each color of chip in the container? (the total number of chips and the number of each color)

As students are analyzing the contents of the container, be sure that students are analyzing the data correctly. If there are 9 red chips, 6 yellow chips, and 3 blue chips, the container contains a total of 18 chips. The probability of choosing red is 9/18 or 1/2, the probability of choosing yellow is 6/18 or 1/3 , and the probability of choosing blue is 3/18 or 1/6.

As students work on What If . . . ? Situations A and B, pay attention to which ideas seem to be difficult for them.

After students have meaningfully found, for instance, that choosing a red or choosing a yellow is the same as not choosing a blue, you might ask the following questions:

  • What is the probability of not blue in What If . . . ? Situation B? How many ways can this occur? (It can occur 4 ways, so the probability is 1 minus all the ways it could happen (1 - (8/12))
  • What happens if you add P(yellow) and P(not yellow) in What If . . .? Situation B? (The sum is one, see the equation below)
  • Continue with this line of questioning for other parts of the problem as needed.

Planning for the Summary

What evidence will you use in the summary to clarify and deepen understanding of the Now What Do You Know? questions? What will you do if you do not have evidence?

Now What Do You Know?

How does experimental probability compare to theoretical probability for a given situation? What can you say about the sum of theoretical probabilities for all possible outcomes?

As you are circulating during the Explore, listen for how students are making sense of experimental and theoretical probabilities. Are they recognizing that experimental probability changes and gets closer to the theoretical probability as more data are collected? Do they see how to use these probabilities to make predictions using proportional reasoning? Do they see the sum of theoretical probabilities for all possible outcome is always 1?
